ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general ramen noodle soup class (FOODON_00002700); no specific mapo-flavoured ramen with egg subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers noodle soup dishes broadly; no mapo ramen-specific code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for mapo ramen or flavoured-egg ramen dishes.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of mapo ramen with flavoured egg, approximately 550 g, comprising noodles, broth, mapo tofu sauce, and one marinated soft-boiled egg.

nutrient_source.title: Ramen noodle soup with pork and tofu — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entries: Ramen noodles cooked (FDC ID 168936), Pork ground cooked (FDC ID 168351), Tofu firm (FDC ID 172476), Egg hard-boiled (FDC ID 173424), and soy-based broth components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for mapo ramen with flavoured egg. Values are estimated by weighted-ingredient composition: approximately 45% cooked ramen noodles, 20% mapo sauce (ground pork, tofu, doubanjiang, sesame oil, soy sauce), 25% pork-based broth, and 10% flavoured egg. Sodium is elevated due to doubanjiang, soy sauce, and seasoned broth. Energy and macros are consistent with published ramen dish analyses in peer-reviewed literature.

gi_source.title: Noodles, instant, boiled — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to mapo ramen noodle component
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mapo ramen with flavoured egg. GI of 55 is estimated based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled wheat noodles / instant noodles (GI range 47–58, mean ~55). The mapo sauce, tofu, egg, and broth components contribute negligible glycemic load. GL per serving calculated as (55 × 70.4 g available carbohydrate per 550 g serving) / 100 = 38.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g; ~2860 mg per bowl) from doubanjiang, soy sauce, and seasoned broth
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid mapo ramen due to its very high sodium content, which can substantially exceed the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg sodium in a single serving; requesting reduced-sodium broth and sauce is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~55) with high glycemic load per serving (~38.7) due to large noodle portion
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious with mapo ramen as the large noodle portion produces a high glycemic load per bowl; portion reduction, choosing lower-GI noodle variants, and pairing with additional protein and vegetables can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium, moderate saturated fat from ground pork and egg yolk, and pro-inflammatory spicy sauce components
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should limit mapo ramen consumption due to its high sodium content and moderate saturated fat from pork and egg, both of which are associated with elevated blood pressure and adverse lipid profiles; occasional consumption with sodium-reduced modifications is preferable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mapo Ramen with Flavoured Egg is a Japanese-Chinese fusion ramen dish that combines the bold, numbing heat of Sichuan-style mapo tofu sauce with a bowl of wheat ramen noodles in a rich pork or chicken broth, topped with a soy-marinated soft-boiled egg (ajitsuke tamago). The dish originates from the creative fusion of Japanese ramen culture with Chinese mapo tofu (麻婆豆腐), a Sichuan preparation featuring silken tofu, ground pork, doubanjiang (spicy fermented bean paste), Sichuan peppercorn, garlic, ginger, and sesame oil. The result is a deeply savoury, spicy, and umami-rich bowl with a silky broth, tender noodles, and a creamy marinated egg that balances the heat. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate protein (~6.2 g per 100 g) from pork, tofu, and egg, with carbohydrates (~12.8 g per 100 g) primarily from wheat noodles. Sodium content is very high (~520 mg per 100 g; approximately 2860 mg per standard 550 g bowl), making it a significant concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (medium), but the glycemic load per serving is high (~38.7) due to the large noodle portion, which is relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding gluten, soy, or egg allergens. It is commonly served as a main meal in Japanese ramen restaurants and is available in Singapore through specialty ramen establishments.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or ICMR-NIN) contains a direct entry for mapo ramen with flavoured egg. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-ingredient composition using multiple USDA FoodData Central entries for component foods. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using boiled wheat noodles as the closest analogous food. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central component entries; no single approved source entry exists for this dish. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component sources used for this estimate. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component sources used for this estimate. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component sources used for this estimate. Trans fat is 0.0; confirmed negligible in the component foods per USDA FoodData Central. Sodium estimate (~520 mg per 100 g) reflects the high-sodium nature of doubanjiang, soy sauce, and seasoned ramen broth; actual restaurant values may vary. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Dairy and eggs' to 'Mixed', which is the appropriate classification for a composite ramen dish. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on boiled wheat noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for mapo ramen exists in approved sources.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.8), sodium_mg (520.0), saturated_fat_g (1.3), and sugar_g (1.2) are all present as numeric values and are plausible for a mapo ramen dish.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (55), gi_category (medium), glycemic_load_per_serving (38.7), and gi_source are all present. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using boiled wheat noodles, with estimation basis fully documented.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn closest_parent (FOODON_00002700, ramen noodle soup) and FoodEx2 closest_parent (A0CKP, noodle soup dish) are defensible. SNOMED CT is null with gap note. No codes are invented. match_type values are all within the allowed set (exact, closest_parent, none).
